content.text: The four main cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, FaaS, and SaaS — exist on a spectrum. As you move from IaaS toward SaaS, AWS takes on progressively more of the operational burden: physical hardware, networking, operating systems, runtimes, and eventually the application itself. Understanding where each model sits on that spectrum, and which one fits a given workload, is one of the most practically useful skills in cloud architecture.
The Shared Responsibility Spectrum

Every cloud service model divides responsibility between you and AWS. The diagram below shows which layers you manage (yellow) versus which layers AWS manages (purple) for each model. Notice how the customer zone shrinks as you move from IaaS to SaaS — but your data and access control remain your responsibility in every model.

attrs.alt: Shared responsibility spectrum across IaaS, PaaS, FaaS, and SaaS showing which layers each party manages
attrs.title: Cloud Service Model Shared Responsibility Spectrum

AWS examples

Elastic Beanstalk, App Runner
Lambda, Step Functions

Choosing the Right Model

No model is universally best. The decision is shaped by four practical factors: the workload's state requirements, its traffic pattern, your team's operational capacity, and the time-to-market priority. Here are the key questions to ask.
Choose IaaS when…

You need specific OS configurations, kernel parameters, or custom security hardening that a PaaS platform cannot expose (e.g., FIPS-validated OS builds, custom eBPF programs, or proprietary device drivers).

You are running stateful workloads that require local disk performance beyond what managed storage offers — high-IOPS databases with custom tuning, HPC clusters with RDMA networking, or machine learning training jobs on bare-metal GPU instances.

You are lifting-and-shifting a legacy application that was built for a specific OS configuration and cannot be replatformed without significant rework. IaaS gives you the same operating environment as an on-premises server.

Your team has strong infrastructure expertise and wants full control over every layer — OS hardening, tunable kernel parameters, custom monitoring agents — and is willing to own the patching burden in exchange.
Choose PaaS when…

You are building a long-running web application or API that needs to be always available and handle a predictable, sustained traffic level. Elastic Beanstalk or App Runner handles load balancing, autoscaling, and health checks without you configuring any of it.
Your app is already containerized (Docker). Containers map cleanly to PaaS deployment targets — push an image, set environment variables, the platform handles the rest.
You want to eliminate OS patching and runtime version management without moving to the stateless model that FaaS requires. PaaS lets you run existing web frameworks (Rails, Django, Spring) as-is.
Choose FaaS when…

The workload is event-driven and bursty — file processing triggered by S3 uploads, webhook receivers, scheduled data transformations, or async processing of SQS messages. Lambda's scale-to-zero means you pay nothing when events are not arriving.

Each unit of work is naturally short and stateless. REST API handlers, data enrichment pipelines, and notification dispatch are good candidates. If the task requires stateful orchestration or exceeds 15 minutes, use Step Functions to chain Lambda invocations.
Your team is small and you want to eliminate all operational overhead beyond function code. With Lambda you never SSH into a server, patch an OS, or configure an autoscaling group.

Traffic is highly variable and unpredictable. A social media feature that spikes to 50,000 requests per second during an event and drops to near zero overnight is a perfect FaaS target. Lambda absorbs the spike automatically and costs nothing during the quiet periods.
Choose SaaS when…

The capability is a commodity business function — email, video conferencing, BI dashboards, CRM — where building it yourself would not be a competitive differentiator. SaaS lets your engineering team focus on what makes your product unique.
Speed to value is paramount. Signing up for a SaaS tool takes minutes; building the equivalent from IaaS takes months. For capabilities needed immediately, SaaS wins on time-to-value.

The vendor's multi-tenant scale gives you capabilities your own implementation could not match — global CDN, compliance certifications (SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA), and 24/7 SLAs backed by large support organizations.
Common Hybrid Architectures
Real architectures rarely use a single service model. Most production systems mix IaaS, PaaS, FaaS, and SaaS based on the requirements of each component. Here are the most common hybrid patterns.
PaaS frontend + FaaS backend.

An App Runner container serves a web frontend with stable traffic patterns. API endpoints that handle file uploads, send emails, or process async tasks are implemented as Lambda functions. App Runner handles the steady-state HTTP load; Lambda handles the burst-friendly async operations.
IaaS database + FaaS compute.

An EC2-hosted or RDS-managed relational database (requiring durable connections and complex SQL) paired with Lambda for the API layer. RDS Proxy sits between Lambda and RDS to multiplex thousands of Lambda execution environments into a small connection pool.
SaaS integration via FaaS.

A Stripe payment webhook arrives at an API Gateway endpoint, triggers a Lambda function that writes a transaction record to DynamoDB, then publishes a Slack notification via the Slack SaaS API. The entire payment-received flow runs without any always-on infrastructure.
IaaS for compliance + SaaS for productivity.

A regulated financial institution runs core banking on dedicated EC2 instances within a VPC to maintain network isolation and meet audit requirements. Internal staff use Amazon WorkMail (SaaS) for email and Amazon Connect (SaaS) for customer service — commodity tools that do not require custom infrastructure.
Cost Model Comparison
The cost structure of each model differs fundamentally. Understanding the difference prevents surprises at the end of the month.
IaaS: fixed baseline + variable storage/bandwidth.
You pay for instance-hours regardless of utilization. A t3.medium at $0.0416/hour costs ~$30/month at 100% idle. Reserved Instances reduce this by 30–60% for predictable workloads.
PaaS: minimum-instance baseline + scale-out per unit.

Elastic Beanstalk requires at least one running instance. App Runner charges $0.064/vCPU-hour for active requests plus $0.007/vCPU-hour when idle — a significant savings over EC2 idle time, but not zero.
FaaS: pure consumption pricing.

Lambda charges $0.20 per 1 million requests plus $0.0000166667 per GB-second. The first 1 million requests and 400,000 GB-seconds per month are free. For intermittent workloads FaaS is nearly always cheaper. For sustained high-throughput workloads (> ~5,000 requests/second around the clock), dedicated compute can become cheaper — this is the "cost inversion" point.
SaaS: subscription per seat or usage tier.

Amazon QuickSight costs $18/author/month (Standard). Amazon Connect charges per minute of inbound call. The cost is predictable and scales with organizational headcount or usage volume rather than technical infrastructure metrics.
Migration Patterns — Moving Up the Stack

Teams often start with IaaS (full control during initial development) and migrate workloads upward as the system matures and operational costs become apparent. The migration is not necessarily all-or-nothing.
IaaS → PaaS (Replatform)

An app running on a manually-managed EC2 instance is containerized (Dockerized) without code changes, then deployed to App Runner or Elastic Beanstalk. The result: the same application code, but AWS now manages OS patching, autoscaling, and load balancing. The engineering team stops running Ansible playbooks for security patches.

# Before: EC2 manual deploy
ssh ec2-user@10.0.0.50
git pull && npm install && pm2 restart app

# After: App Runner from ECR image
aws apprunner create-service \
  --service-name my-api \
  --source-configuration '{
    "ImageRepository": {
      "ImageIdentifier": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest",
      "ImageRepositoryType": "ECR"
    },
    "AutoDeploymentsEnabled": true
  }' \
  --instance-configuration '{"Cpu": "1 vCPU", "Memory": "2 GB"}'
PaaS → FaaS (Re-architect)

Moving from PaaS to FaaS requires re-architecting stateful request handlers into stateless, single-purpose functions. This is a meaningful code change. Common triggers for this migration: traffic dropped to near-zero for long periods (making PaaS idle cost painful) or a specific endpoint became the bottleneck and needs independent scaling.

# Before: Express/Flask route handler (PaaS — stateful in-process cache)
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
_cache = {}  # in-process state — breaks with Lambda's per-environment isolation

@app.route('/orders', methods=['POST'])
def create_order():
    body = request.get_json()
    _cache[body['id']] = body  # NOT safe in FaaS
    return jsonify({'id': body['id']}), 201

# After: Lambda handler (FaaS — stateless, external state in DynamoDB)
import json, boto3
table = boto3.resource('dynamodb').Table('Orders')  # module-scope init

def handler(event, context):
    body = json.loads(event['body'])
    table.put_item(Item={'orderId': body['id'], 'status': 'created'})
    return {'statusCode': 201, 'body': json.dumps({'id': body['id']})}
Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Running a monolith on Lambda.

Packaging a 200 MB Express application as a single Lambda function gets you a slow cold start, high memory use, and none of the independent-scaling benefits of FaaS. Use PaaS (App Runner, ECS) for monoliths.
Choosing IaaS for everything to avoid learning new services.

Running Elasticsearch on EC2 instead of using Amazon OpenSearch Service (a PaaS equivalent) means you own cluster upgrades, disk management, and shard rebalancing. The time cost of that operational work usually exceeds the savings from EC2 pricing.
Using SaaS where data residency is non-negotiable.

Some regulated industries (healthcare, government, financial services) require data to remain within specific jurisdictions. SaaS multi-tenancy can complicate data residency compliance. In those cases, PaaS or IaaS with explicit region pinning is safer than SaaS.
Lambda for long-running batch jobs.
A nightly 4-hour database export cannot run on Lambda (15-minute max). Use AWS Batch (managed HPC on EC2/Fargate) or ECS Fargate tasks for long-running, memory-intensive batch work.
A Decision Framework
When evaluating a new workload, walk through these four questions in order:
Can a SaaS product solve this?
If yes, default to SaaS. The total cost of ownership (licensing + integration time) is almost always lower than building from IaaS for commodity capabilities.
Is the workload event-driven and short-lived (< 15 min)?
If yes, consider FaaS first. Evaluate whether the traffic pattern is bursty or has long idle periods — FaaS excels here.
Is the workload always-on and stateless?
If yes, PaaS (App Runner or Elastic Beanstalk). Containerize the app and let the platform handle the rest.
Does the workload require custom OS configuration, specific hardware, or stateful storage tightly coupled to the compute node?
If yes, IaaS. Accept the operational overhead in exchange for the control.
AWS Services Mapped to Each Model

AWS offers hundreds of services. Knowing which service model category each one belongs to helps you reason about its operational overhead and your responsibility boundary before you add it to an architecture.
IaaS Services — You Manage the OS and Up
EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)

— Virtual machines in any size from t4g.nano (2 vCPU, 0.5 GB, $0.004/hr) to u-24tb1.112xlarge (448 vCPU, 24 TB, bare-metal). You choose the AMI, configure the OS, install software, and manage security patches.
EBS (Elastic Block Store)

— Persistent block storage volumes (SSD or HDD) attached to EC2 instances. You partition, format, mount, and manage the filesystem. EBS handles physical disk replication within an AZ; the rest is yours.
VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)

— Isolated network you design: subnets, route tables, internet gateways, NAT gateways, security groups, and NACLs. You define the IP address space and control all traffic flows. AWS provides the underlying switching and routing fabric.
EC2 Auto Scaling + ELB

— Horizontal scaling for EC2 fleets. You define the AMI, scaling policies, health checks, and target groups. AWS scales the fleet and routes traffic, but you wrote the policies that govern when and how much to scale.
PaaS Services — You Manage the App and Data
Elastic Beanstalk

— Deploy Node.js, Python, Java, .NET, PHP, Ruby, or Docker applications by uploading a ZIP or container image. Beanstalk provisions EC2 instances, ELB, Auto Scaling, and CloudWatch alarms. You configure environment variables, instance type, and the platform version; AWS runs the infrastructure.
AWS App Runner

— Zero-configuration container deployment. Point App Runner at an ECR image or a GitHub repository and it builds, deploys, scales, and load-balances automatically. There are no EC2 instances to configure, no ALB listeners to define, and no Auto Scaling groups to size.
Amazon RDS

— Managed relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, Db2). AWS provisions the instance, installs the database engine, runs automated backups and point-in-time recovery, and manages OS patches. You configure the parameter group, schema, and queries.
Amazon ElastiCache
— Managed Redis and Valkey (open-source Redis fork) caching. AWS handles cluster provisioning, failover, patching, and backup. You configure the node type, cluster mode, and eviction policy.
Amazon OpenSearch Service

— Managed Elasticsearch / OpenSearch clusters for full-text search and log analytics. AWS provisions nodes, handles upgrades, and runs automated snapshots. You design the index mappings and write the queries.
FaaS and Serverless Services — You Manage Code and Config
AWS Lambda

— The FaaS primitive. Write a handler function in Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, or Ruby; configure memory and timeout; define a trigger. Lambda handles provisioning, scaling, and runtime patching. You pay per invocation and per GB-second of execution.
Amazon DynamoDB

— Serverless NoSQL key-value and document database. No instances to provision; capacity scales automatically in On-Demand mode. You model the table schema (partition key, sort key, GSIs) and pay per read/write unit consumed.
Amazon API Gateway
— Managed HTTP and WebSocket API routing. No servers to run; AWS handles TLS termination, throttling, caching, and routing rules. You define routes, integrations, and authorizers.
AWS Step Functions

— Serverless workflow orchestration. Define a state machine (JSON or YAML) that coordinates Lambda functions, ECS tasks, DynamoDB operations, and external HTTP calls with built-in retry, error handling, and parallel execution. AWS manages the orchestration engine; you write the workflow definition.
Amazon S3

— Serverless object storage. No storage nodes to manage; capacity is unlimited and scales automatically. You set bucket policies, lifecycle rules, and versioning. S3 is both a data store and an event source — uploads trigger Lambda directly.
Amazon SQS / SNS / EventBridge

— Serverless messaging. SQS queues messages with at-least-once delivery and configurable visibility timeouts. SNS fans a message out to multiple subscribers (Lambda, HTTP endpoints, email, SQS). EventBridge routes events from AWS services, custom apps, and SaaS partners to Lambda or other targets. All three scale transparently with no cluster to manage.
SaaS Services — AWS Provides, You Consume
Amazon QuickSight

— Business intelligence dashboards and ML-powered analytics. Connect to S3, RDS, Redshift, or Salesforce; build visualizations; share with users. You manage your data sources and report permissions; AWS operates the SPICE query engine, CDN delivery, and BI infrastructure.
Amazon Connect

— Cloud contact center (inbound/outbound calling, IVR flows, real-time agent dashboards). You configure contact flows and routing profiles; AWS operates the telephony infrastructure, voice recording, and real-time analytics. Pricing is per minute of active call, not per seat-hour.
Amazon WorkMail

— Managed corporate email and calendar (IMAP/SMTP compatible, integrates with Microsoft Outlook and mobile clients). You manage user accounts and mail policies; AWS operates the mail servers, spam filtering, and storage.
AWS Marketplace SaaS

— Third-party SaaS products billed through the AWS bill: Datadog (observability), Splunk (log analytics), Snowflake (data warehouse), MongoDB Atlas, and hundreds more. You consume the SaaS interface; the vendor operates the application infrastructure, potentially using AWS in the background.
Compliance and Security by Model
Moving up the service model stack changes what you are responsible for auditing and securing. Understanding these boundaries is critical for regulated industries.
IaaS: broadest attack surface, full audit scope.

You own OS hardening, patch management, antivirus, log collection, and network security group rules. In a PCI DSS audit, every EC2 instance running cardholder data is in scope for OS-level controls. This gives you maximum control but maximum audit scope.
PaaS: reduced OS audit scope, application still in scope.

RDS removes OS patching from your scope — you verify that AWS is patching through their SOC 2 or ISO 27001 reports (available in AWS Artifact). Application-level controls (SQL injection prevention, access logging, parameter groups) remain your responsibility.
FaaS: narrowest audit scope for compute.

With Lambda you audit IAM execution roles, environment variable encryption, and function code. You do not audit OS patches or network interface configurations. However, Lambda's ephemeral execution environment means there is no persistent filesystem audit trail unless you explicitly write logs to CloudWatch or S3.
SaaS: data residency and tenant isolation are your main concerns.

You cannot audit the SaaS provider's infrastructure directly. Instead, require SOC 2 Type II reports, GDPR Data Processing Agreements, and clear data residency commitments. Understand the multi-tenancy model: are your data and your neighbor's data fully isolated at the storage level, or only logically separated by row-level filters?
IAM is your responsibility in every model.
Regardless of the service model, you control IAM users, roles, policies, and access keys. A misconfigured IAM policy on a Lambda function (granting
dynamodb:*
instead of
dynamodb:PutItem
) exposes the same blast radius whether the compute is EC2, Beanstalk, Lambda, or a SaaS API caller.
Where the Lines Blur: Serverless IaaS and Managed Containers

The model boundaries are not always clean. Several AWS services occupy the space between categories, giving you varying amounts of control without requiring you to commit fully to one end of the spectrum.
AWS Fargate

sits between IaaS and PaaS. You write a Docker container (IaaS-like control over the application environment) but do not manage EC2 instances (PaaS-like infrastructure abstraction). Fargate provisions the compute, patches the hypervisor, and runs your containers on demand. You define CPU, memory, networking, and IAM roles for the task; AWS manages the underlying host fleet.
Amazon Aurora Serverless v2

sits between PaaS and FaaS. It is a managed relational database (PostgreSQL or MySQL compatible — PaaS) that scales compute capacity in fine-grained increments from 0.5 ACUs to 128 ACUs within seconds (FaaS-like elasticity). Unlike FaaS, it does not scale to zero by default but can pause after a configurable idle period. You write SQL (same as standard RDS); the scaling engine is transparent.
AWS Lambda container images

blur the boundary between PaaS containers and FaaS functions. You package a Lambda function as a Docker image (up to 10 GB) with full control over the OS base layer, installed packages, and binary dependencies — more like a container than a zip file. The execution model is still Lambda (event-driven, per-invocation billing, 15-minute max), but the deployment unit looks like a container.
Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)

is IaaS with a managed control plane. You manage worker nodes (EC2 instances with full OS access) and write Kubernetes manifests. AWS manages the Kubernetes API server, etcd cluster, and control plane upgrades. EKS with managed node groups shifts OS patching closer to PaaS, but you still manage the worker node lifecycle, taint/toleration configuration, and cluster add-ons.

The practical conclusion: the IaaS / PaaS / FaaS / SaaS taxonomy is a useful decision framework, not a rigid classification. When AWS introduces a new service, ask "what layer is AWS managing for me, and what am I still responsible for?" That question is more useful than trying to label the service with a single model acronym.
Real-World Scenario: A Startup's Journey
Follow a startup as it evolves its infrastructure from a manually-managed EC2 instance to a mature serverless and SaaS-native architecture over three years.
Year 1 — IaaS (Prototype)

The founders launch on a single t3.medium EC2 instance running Ubuntu. Everything lives on one box: Nginx, Node.js, PostgreSQL, and Redis. They SSH in to deploy. They manage SSL certificates manually with Certbot. Monthly cost: ~$30. Ops overhead: high — every security patch is an SSH session, every deployment is manual.
Year 2 — PaaS Migration (Growth)

After a security incident caused by an unpatched OpenSSL vulnerability, the team containerizes the Node.js app and moves to App Runner. PostgreSQL moves to RDS (eliminating manual backups and OS patches). Redis moves to ElastiCache. The monolith still exists, but AWS now manages OS patching, autoscaling from 1 to 10 instances, and load balancing. Monthly cost: ~$150. Ops overhead: medium — the team focuses on app code and deployment pipelines, not OS administration.
Year 3 — Hybrid FaaS + SaaS (Scale)

Traffic grows 10×. The team identifies three high-volume, stateless API endpoints (image resize, email send, report generation) and extracts them to Lambda functions. S3 stores user uploads. SES handles transactional email (replacing a self-managed Postfix server). QuickSight gives the business team self-service analytics dashboards without a data engineering build. The App Runner monolith still runs the core product UI.

The result: a hybrid architecture where the right model is applied to each component. The image resize Lambda costs $4/month at 5 million invocations. The same workload on a dedicated EC2 t3.large would cost $60/month. The SES email service eliminates a postmaster who was spending 20% of their time managing bounce rates and SPF/DKIM records. This is the practical outcome of matching service model to workload.
Key Takeaways
IaaS, PaaS, FaaS, and SaaS form a spectrum from maximum control (IaaS) to minimum operational overhead (SaaS). As you move right, AWS manages more layers but your flexibility decreases.
Your data and access control remain your responsibility in every model. Nothing on the spectrum absolves you of securing what you store or controlling who can access it.
Cost models differ fundamentally: IaaS charges for idle time, PaaS has a minimum baseline, FaaS charges only for actual invocations (scale to zero), and SaaS charges per seat or usage tier.
Real architectures mix models. A single production system often uses SaaS for email, FaaS for event processing, PaaS for the web tier, and IaaS for the compliance-sensitive database layer.
The decision framework: SaaS first (commodity), FaaS second (bursty/event-driven), PaaS third (always-on stateless), IaaS last (custom hardware or OS control required).
